title: Team Onboarding
text: Set up one shared base where each member can own one prototype module while keeping style and structure consistent.

title: Team Onboarding
text: Set up one shared base where each member can own one prototype module while keeping style and structure consistent.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Suggested Team Split

Member A: EDA + confirmatory module.
Member B: cluster module.
Member C: decision tree module.
Member D (optional): integration, QA, and deployment.

title: Team Onboarding
text:
Branching Convention

Create branches as codex/<module-name> or feature/<module-name>.
Keep pull requests scoped to one module/page at a time.
Merge only after quarto render and app smoke-test pass.

git clone <repo-url>
cd group_project
quarto preview

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
This page is structured as an individual Take-home Exercise 2 submission and focuses on one prototype module:
Selected module: Cluster Analysis for monthly tourism market states.
The module uses monthly observations to identify interpretable tourism states:

Pandemic shock months.
Recovery transition months.
High-performance months.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
This page is structured as an individual Take-home Exercise 2 submission and focuses on one prototype module:
Selected module: Cluster Analysis for monthly tourism market states.
The module uses monthly observations to identify interpretable tourism states:

Pandemic shock months.
Recovery transition months.
High-performance months.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
1. Data Preparation Process
Data preparation follows the imported bilingual plan and detailed cleaning notes in data/references/.
Key preparation decisions:

Remove metadata rows and invalid 2015-12-01 record.
Use monthly variables for model features.
Keep annual variables only for supplementary visual context.
Create china_share, period, and calendar fields (year, month, quarter).
Use capped stay-length and z-score fields for stable clustering.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
5. UI Design
UI design principles in this prototype:

Progressive disclosure: show defaults first, advanced controls optional.
Interpretability-first: output labels use domain terms, not only model terms.
Comparability: consistent color mapping between plot and table.

Detailed storyboard and component mapping are provided at UI Storyboard.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
6. Visual Design & Interactivity Practices
This prototype applies:

Clear visual hierarchy for decision steps.
Explicit mapping between controls and outputs.
Responsive layout for laptop and mobile viewport.
Data-informed defaults to reduce trial-and-error.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
7. Current Status and Next Step
Completed:

Modular app skeleton.
Data integration and smoke-test artifacts.
Quarto write-up scaffold aligned to assignment requirements.

Next:

Add confirmatory stats module.
Add decision-tree module with feature importance panel.
Add deployment pipeline for GitHub Pages + hosted Shiny endpoint.

title: Project Home
text:
Visual Analytics · Quarto + Shiny · AY2025/26





How can one shared tourism time-series dataset support exploration, recovery-state clustering, and forecasting for Singapore’s tourism recovery?
This site is an integrated workspace for:

Take-home Exercise 2 write-up.
Prototype module design and validation.
Team collaboration and onboarding.
Linked Shiny module development built around a three-module time-series app.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and the share of the Chinese source market all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to explore these structural shifts from multiple perspectives. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to help users understand how Singapore’s tourism market moved from pandemic shock toward recovery and high-performance states.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and the share of the Chinese source market all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to explore these structural shifts from multiple perspectives. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to help users understand how Singapore’s tourism market moved from pandemic shock toward recovery and high-performance states.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
2. Problem Statement 
Current discussions on tourism recovery often focus narrowly on whether visitor numbers have returned, without adequately examining market structure, seasonality, or stage-specific differences in recovery. In particular, there is a lack of an interpretable and interactive analytical framework to show how the Chinese source market contributed to Singapore’s overall tourism recovery and whether hotel recovery was mainly driven by total demand volume.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
3. Project Aim
The project aims to develop a visual analytics prototype based on monthly tourism data, enabling users to understand tourism recovery at descriptive, statistical, and model-based levels. The prototype will integrate exploratory data analysis, confirmatory analysis, clustering, decision tree analysis, and random forest analysis to explain how tourism market states evolve over time.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
4. The Data
The project data is sourced from the CEIC website and is guaranteed to be reliable in terms of data origin. We selected the following sets of data for our research.

Average Length of Stay
Hotel Room Occupancy Rate
Tourist Expenditure Per Capit
Tourism Receipts
Average Length of Stay
Visitor Arrivals
Visitor Arrivals: China

The project uses a cleaned monthly tourism dataset spanning December 2016 to January 2026. Core variables include total visitor arrivals, visitor arrivals from China, hotel room occupancy rate, monthly average length of stay, and derived fields such as China’s visitor share, quarter, year, and COVID-stage labels. Annual variables such as tourism receipts and expenditure per capita will be used only as supplementary context rather than as direct inputs for the monthly classification models.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
5Research Questions
The project focuses on the following questions:

How did Singapore’s tourism market differ across the pre-COVID, shock, and recovery periods?
Did the recovery of the Chinese source market move in tandem with the broader tourism market?
Which factors best determine whether a month falls into a low, medium, or high hotel occupancy state?
Can monthly tourism observations be grouped into meaningful market-state clusters?

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
6. Methodology and Analytical Approach
The project adopts four main analytical modules. First, exploratory analysis will reveal trends, seasonality, and unusual shifts. Second, confirmatory analysis will validate stage differences through correlation analysis, group comparisons, and significance testing. Third, clustering will classify months into interpretable market states such as “pandemic shock,” “recovery transition,” and “high-performance.” Finally, tree-based models, including decision trees and random forests, will identify the main drivers of hotel occupancy levels.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
7. Decision Tree and Random Forest
The decision tree module emphasizes interpretability by translating complex relationships into clear rules, such as identifying thresholds below which months are likely to fall into low occupancy states. The random forest module serves as a more robust nonlinear classifier for assessing variable importance and testing model stability on unseen data. Together, the two approaches support both explanation and validation. Preliminary prototype testing suggests that total visitor volume is the most stable key variable, while seasonality, length of stay, and China’s visitor share provide additional discriminating power.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
8Data Visualisation Methods
The prototype will include time-series line charts, period-based boxplots, correlation heatmaps, cluster scatter plots, cluster profile charts, decision tree visualizations, random forest variable importance plots, confusion-matrix heatmaps, and model-performance-versus-tree-count plots. These visualizations are designed not merely to display results, but to reveal the layered logic, threshold effects, and state transitions underlying tourism recovery.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
9 R Packages







Package
Description




tidyverse
Used for data cleaning, transformation, filtering, summarisation, and general data manipulation.


readxl
Used to import the original Excel dataset into R.


lubridate
Used to process date variables and create year, month, and quarter fields.


writexl
Used to export cleaned data into Excel format.


ggplot2
Used to create core statistical graphics such as line charts, boxplots, scatterplots, and bar charts.


plotly
Used to add interactivity to selected visualisations.


DT
Used to display interactive data tables in the prototype.


corrplot
Used to visualise correlation matrices among tourism variables.


cluster
Used for clustering analysis and cluster quality assessment such as silhouette scores.


factoextra
Used to visualise clustering results, elbow plots, and cluster profiles.


rpart
Used to build decision tree classification models.


rpart.plot
Used to create clear static decision tree visualisations for reporting.


visNetwork
Used to create interactive tree visualisations.


caret
Used for model training, tuning, resampling, confusion matrix evaluation, and workflow standardisation.


ranger
Used to build random forest classification models efficiently.


patchwork
Used to combine multiple ggplot charts into one display layout.


htmlwidgets
Used to save and render interactive HTML-based outputs.


sparkline
Used to support the interactive decision tree visualisation built with visNetwork.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
The app now follows a three-module structure built on one shared tourism time-series workbook:

app/app.R - top-level navigation and shared reactive data loading.
app/R/data_utils.R - common time-series ingestion, feature engineering, and forecasting helpers.
app/R/mod_cluster_ui.R and app/R/mod_cluster_server.R - time-series clustering module.
app/R/mod_forecast_ui.R and app/R/mod_forecast_server.R - forecasting module.
The Time Series Explorer is embedded directly in app/app.R to keep the main comparison flow simple.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
The app now follows a three-module structure built on one shared tourism time-series workbook:

app/app.R - top-level navigation and shared reactive data loading.
app/R/data_utils.R - common time-series ingestion, feature engineering, and forecasting helpers.
app/R/mod_cluster_ui.R and app/R/mod_cluster_server.R - time-series clustering module.
app/R/mod_forecast_ui.R and app/R/mod_forecast_server.R - forecasting module.
The Time Series Explorer is embedded directly in app/app.R to keep the main comparison flow simple.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
Run Commands
Rscript run_app.R 3838

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
What to Expect
The final app should let the user:

Explore the raw tourism series.
Group months into recovery states.
Generate and compare forecasts for the selected target series.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
One-Click Dev Start (PowerShell)
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File scripts/start-dev.ps1

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
Data Source
The app reads:
data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx
This workbook is treated as the common source for all three modules so that users do not need to switch between inconsistent datasets.
Within that shared workbook, the forecasting tab treats country-level visitor arrivals as the primary target series and uses hotel/stay indicators as supporting context rather than separate forecast targets.

title: Module Prototype: Cluster Analysis
text: Prototype a Shiny module that groups country-level visitor-arrivals trajectories into interpretable time-series clusters.

title: Module Prototype: Cluster Analysis
text: Prototype a Shiny module that groups country-level visitor-arrivals trajectories into interpretable time-series clusters.

title: Module Prototype: Cluster Analysis
text:
Data Contract
Input dataset: data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx
Required fields:

Visitor Arrivals: China
Visitor Arrivals: Malaysia
Visitor Arrivals: India
Visitor Arrivals: Indonesia
Visitor Arrivals: Australia
date

Supporting hotel indicators remain in the shared workbook for explorer and forecasting context, but the cluster prototype operates on the country-arrivals trajectories themselves.

title: Module Prototype: Cluster Analysis
text:
Analytical Design

Load the shared monthly tourism workbook.
Restrict the catalog to country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Select a common lookback window across countries.
Align the country series by month and drop incomplete overlapping dates.
Optionally normalize each country trajectory.
Run kmeans across country trajectories.
Compute silhouette score.
Use PCA only as a 2D projection for display.

title: Module Prototype: Cluster Analysis
text:
Quality Gates

At least two country-arrivals series must be selected.
At least 12 overlapping monthly observations must remain after alignment.
Chosen k must be smaller than the number of selected series.
Country labels must stay visible in the clustering output.

1.1Install and launch the required R packages


Show the code
if (!requireNamespace("pacman", quietly = TRUE)) {

}

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.3Read the original data


Show the code
col_names <- c(
  "date",
  "avg_stay_monthly",
  "hotel_occ",
  "spend_per_capita",
  "tourism_receipts",
  "avg_stay_annual",
  "visitor_arrivals",
  "visitor_arrivals_china"
)

raw_tbl <- readxl::read_excel(
  path = raw_path,
  sheet = "My Series",
  skip = 29,
  col_names = FALSE
) %>%
  setNames(col_names) %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    across(-date, as.numeric)
  ) %>%
  arrange(date)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis
text:
1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis


Show the code
monthly_clean <- raw_tbl %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(date),
    !is.na(avg_stay_monthly),
    !is.na(hotel_occ),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals_china)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date),
    quarter = lubridate::quarter(date),
    period = case_when(
      date <= as.Date("2020-01-01") ~ "pre_covid",
      date <= as.Date("2021-12-01") ~ "covid_shock",
      TRUE ~ "recovery"
    ),
    china_share = visitor_arrivals_china / visitor_arrivals,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.6Handling extreme values


Show the code
stay_cap <- quantile(monthly_clean$avg_stay_monthly, probs = 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

monthly_clean <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    avg_stay_monthly_capped = pmin(avg_stay_monthly, stay_cap),
    visitor_arrivals_millions = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    visitor_arrivals_china_thousands = visitor_arrivals_china / 1000
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.7Generate analysis fields


Show the code
occ_cuts <- quantile(monthly_clean$hotel_occ, probs = c(1/3, 2/3), na.rm = TRUE)

analysis_ready <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    cluster_z_visitor_arrivals = as.numeric(scale(visitor_arrivals)),
    cluster_z_china_share = as.numeric(scale(china_share)),
    cluster_z_hotel_occ = as.numeric(scale(hotel_occ)),
    cluster_z_avg_stay_monthly_capped = as.numeric(scale(avg_stay_monthly_capped)),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = case_when(
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[1]] ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[2]] ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    ),
    hotel_occ_level_business = case_when(
      hotel_occ < 70 ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= 85 ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(hotel_occ_level_tertile, levels = c("low", "medium", "high")),
    hotel_occ_level_business = factor(hotel_occ_level_business, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    dataset_split = if_else(row_number() <= floor(n() * 0.8), "train", "test")
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 1.8Generate data specifically for classification tree construction
text:
1.8Generate data specifically for classification tree construction


Show the code
decision_tree_ready <- analysis_ready %>%
  select(
    date, year, month, quarter, period,
    visitor_arrivals, visitor_arrivals_china,
    china_share, china_share_pct,
    hotel_occ, avg_stay_monthly, avg_stay_monthly_capped,
    hotel_occ_level_tertile, hotel_occ_level_business,
    dataset_split
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.9Export the cleaned data


Show the code
readr::write_csv(monthly_clean, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_monthly_clean.csv"))
readr::write_csv(decision_tree_ready, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_decision_tree_ready.csv"))
readr::write_csv(analysis_ready, file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_four_part_analysis_ready.csv"))

writexl::write_xlsx(
  list(
    monthly_clean = monthly_clean,
    decision_tree_ready = decision_tree_ready,
    analysis_ready_all = analysis_ready
  ),
  path = file.path(processed_dir, "tourism_four_part_analysis_ready.xlsx")
)

dir.create(output_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.2Reading data


Show the code
df <- readr::read_csv(data_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    month = factor(
      month,
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = c("Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
                 "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec")
    ),
    quarter = factor(quarter),
    period = factor(period, levels = c("pre_covid", "covid_shock", "recovery")),
    dataset_split = factor(dataset_split, levels = c("train", "test")),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(
      hotel_occ_level_tertile,
      levels = c("low", "medium", "high")
    ),

    # 为了让树图上的阈值更易读，创建展示型变量
    arrivals_million = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100,
    stay_days = avg_stay_monthly_capped
  )

glimpse(df)


Rows: 110
Columns: 17
$ date                     <date> 2016-12-01, 2017-01-01, 2017-02-01, 2017-03-…
$ year                     <dbl> 2016, 2017, 2017, 2017, 2017, 2017, 2017, 201…
$ month                    <fct> Dec, Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, …
$ quarter                  <fct> 4, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 1, 1, …
$ period                   <fct> pre_covid, pre_covid, pre_covid, pre_covid, p…
$ visitor_arrivals         <dbl> 1506134, 1480479, 1361010, 1480161, 1470750, …
$ visitor_arrivals_china   <dbl> 226020, 316805, 268993, 264711, 252135, 23236…
$ china_share              <dbl> 0.1500663, 0.2139882, 0.1976422, 0.1788393, 0…
$ china_share_pct          <dbl> 15.00663, 21.39882, 19.76422, 17.88393, 17.14…
$ hotel_occ                <dbl> 79.61207, 81.76737, 88.02775, 84.67201, 85.06…
$ avg_stay_monthly         <dbl> 3.214346, 3.517353, 3.423516, 3.150551, 3.280…
$ avg_stay_monthly_capped  <dbl> 3.214346, 3.517353, 3.423516, 3.150551, 3.280…
$ hotel_occ_level_tertile  <fct> medium, medium, high, high, high, medium, med…
$ hotel_occ_level_business <chr> "medium", "medium", "high", "medium", "high",…
$ dataset_split            <fct> train, train, train, train, train, train, tra…
$ arrivals_million         <dbl> 1.506134, 1.480479, 1.361010, 1.480161, 1.470…
$ stay_days                <dbl> 3.214346, 3.517353, 3.423516, 3.150551, 3.280…

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 2.3Split the dataset into training and testing sets
text:
2.3Split the dataset into training and testing sets


Show the code
train_df <- df %>% filter(dataset_split == "train")
test_df  <- df %>% filter(dataset_split == "test")

cat("Train rows:", nrow(train_df), "\n")


Train rows: 88 


Show the code
cat("Test rows:", nrow(test_df), "\n")


Test rows: 22

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.4Build classification tree
Target variable: Hotel occupancy rate level (low / medium / high)
Independent variables: # arrivals_million = Total inbound tourists (in millions)
china_share_pct = Proportion of Chinese tourists (percentage)
stay_days = Truncated average length of stay
month = Month (seasonality)


Show the code
fit_tree <- rpart::rpart(
  hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + china_share_pct + stay_days + month,
  data = train_df,
  method = "class",
  parms = list(split = "gini"),
  control = rpart::rpart.control(
    cp = 0.005,
    maxdepth = 4,
    minsplit = 10,
    minbucket = 5,
    xval = 10
  )
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.5Check the complexity parameter table.


Show the code
printcp(fit_tree)



Classification tree:
rpart::rpart(formula = hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + 
    china_share_pct + stay_days + month, data = train_df, method = "class", 
    parms = list(split = "gini"), control = rpart::rpart.control(cp = 0.005, 
        maxdepth = 4, minsplit = 10, minbucket = 5, xval = 10))

Variables actually used in tree construction:
[1] arrivals_million month           

Root node error: 57/88 = 0.64773

n= 88 

       CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
1 0.49123      0   1.00000 1.17544 0.070151
2 0.21053      1   0.50877 0.52632 0.078011
3 0.04386      2   0.29825 0.38596 0.071263
4 0.00500      4   0.21053 0.42105 0.073296


Show the code
plotcp(fit_tree)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.6Prune the tree


Show the code
bestcp <- fit_tree$cptable[which.min(fit_tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
pruned_tree <- prune(fit_tree, cp = bestcp)

cat("Best cp:", bestcp, "\n")


Best cp: 0.04385965 


Show the code
print(pruned_tree)


n= 88 

node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
      * denotes terminal node

1) root 88 57 low (0.35227273 0.29545455 0.35227273)  
  2) arrivals_million< 0.7575905 31  3 low (0.90322581 0.09677419 0.00000000) *
  3) arrivals_million>=0.7575905 57 26 high (0.05263158 0.40350877 0.54385965)  
    6) month=Jan,May,Dec 16  3 medium (0.12500000 0.81250000 0.06250000) *
    7) month=Feb,Mar,Apr,Jun,Jul,Aug,Sep,Oct,Nov 41 11 high (0.02439024 0.24390244 0.73170732) *

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.7Interactive tree visualization


Show the code
tree_vis <- visNetwork::visTree(
  pruned_tree,
  data = as.data.frame(train_df),
  main = "Decision Tree for Hotel Occupancy Level",
  submain = "Target: low / medium / high",
  width = "100%",
  height = "700px",
  nodesFontSize = 16,
  edgesFontSize = 14,
  fallenLeaves = TRUE,
  legend = TRUE,
  nodesPopSize = TRUE,
  minNodeSize = 14,
  maxNodeSize = 32,
  highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, hover = TRUE),
  export = TRUE
)

tree_vis








Show the code
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(
  tree_vis,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_visual.html"),
  selfcontained = FALSE
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.9Predict on test set


Show the code
pred_class <- predict(pruned_tree, newdata = test_df, type = "class")
pred_prob  <- predict(pruned_tree, newdata = test_df, type = "prob") %>%
  as.data.frame()

# 统一 levels，避免 confusionMatrix 报错
pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile <- factor(
  test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile,
  levels = c("low", "medium", "high")
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.10Evaluate model performance


Show the code
cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(
  data = pred_class,
  reference = test_df$hotel_occ_level_tertile
)

print(cm)


Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction low medium high
    low      0      0    0
    medium   2      4    0
    high     4      6    6

Overall Statistics
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.4545          
                 95% CI : (0.2439, 0.6779)
    No Information Rate : 0.4545          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.581967        
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.1951          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.007383        

Statistics by Class:

                     Class: low Class: medium Class: high
Sensitivity              0.0000        0.4000      1.0000
Specificity              1.0000        0.8333      0.3750
Pos Pred Value              NaN        0.6667      0.3750
Neg Pred Value           0.7273        0.6250      1.0000
Prevalence               0.2727        0.4545      0.2727
Detection Rate           0.0000        0.1818      0.2727
Detection Prevalence     0.0000        0.2727      0.7273
Balanced Accuracy        0.5000        0.6167      0.6875


Show the code
metrics_tbl <- tibble(
  metric = c("accuracy", "kappa"),
  value = c(cm$overall[["Accuracy"]], cm$overall[["Kappa"]])
)

print(metrics_tbl)


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  metric   value
  <chr>    <dbl>

2 kappa    0.195

text:

Show the code
importance_tbl <- tibble(

) %>%

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
2.12Export results


Show the code
write.csv(
  as.data.frame(cm$table),
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_confusion_matrix.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  metrics_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_metrics.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

write.csv(
  importance_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_variable_importance.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

prediction_tbl <- bind_cols(
  test_df %>% select(date, hotel_occ, hotel_occ_level_tertile),
  tibble(predicted_class = pred_class),
  pred_prob
)

write.csv(
  prediction_tbl,
  file.path(output_dir, "decision_tree_test_predictions.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

cat("Decision tree analysis complete.\n")

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.4Confusion matrix heatmap


Show the code
conf_tbl <- as.data.frame(cm$table)

conf_plot <- ggplot(conf_tbl, aes(x = Reference, y = Prediction, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f7fbff", high = "#08519c") +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Confusion Matrix",
    x = "Actual class",
    y = "Predicted class",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(conf_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_confusion_heatmap.png"),
  plot = conf_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 200
)

text:
3.5Variable importance plot


Show the code
importance_plot <- importance_tbl %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Overall, n = 10) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, Overall), y = Overall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7fb8") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Variable Importance",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.6OOB accuracy vs number of trees


Show the code
tree_grid <- seq(50, 300, by = 25)

oob_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(tree_grid, function(nt) {
  mod <- ranger::ranger(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + china_share_pct + stay_days + month,
    data = train_df,
    num.trees = nt,
    mtry = rf_model$bestTune$mtry,
    min.node.size = rf_model$bestTune$min.node.size,
    splitrule = as.character(rf_model$bestTune$splitrule),
    importance = "impurity",
    seed = 1234
  )

  tibble(
    num_trees = nt,
    oob_error = mod$prediction.error,
    oob_accuracy = 1 - mod$prediction.error
  )
})

print(oob_tbl)


# A tibble: 11 × 3
   num_trees oob_error oob_accuracy
       <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
 1        50     0.318        0.682
 2        75     0.318        0.682
 3       100     0.307        0.693
 4       125     0.295        0.705
 5       150     0.284        0.716
 6       175     0.284        0.716
 7       200     0.295        0.705
 8       225     0.295        0.705
 9       250     0.295        0.705
10       275     0.341        0.659
11       300     0.330        0.670


Show the code
oob_plot <- ggplot(oob_tbl, aes(x = num_trees, y = oob_accuracy)) +
  geom_line(color = "#238b45", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#238b45", size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "OOB Accuracy vs Number of Trees",
    x = "Number of trees",
    y = "OOB accuracy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(oob_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_oob_accuracy.png"),
  plot = oob_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.7Actual vs predicted over time


Show the code
pred_plot_df <- test_df %>%
  transmute(
    date,
    actual = hotel_occ_level_tertile,
    predicted = pred_class
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(actual, predicted),
    names_to = "series",
    values_to = "level"
  )

pred_plot <- ggplot(pred_plot_df, aes(x = date, y = level, color = series, shape = series)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs Predicted Hotel Occupancy Level",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Occupancy level"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(actual = "#d95f0e", predicted = "#3182bd")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

print(pred_plot)









Show the code
ggsave(
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "random_forest_actual_vs_predicted.png"),
  plot = pred_plot,
  width = 9,
  height = 5,
  dpi = 200
)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
source("../app/R/data_utils.R")
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(DT)
library(lubridate)
library(timetk)
library(modeltime)
library(tidymodels)
library(tsibble)
library(feasts)
options(scipen = 999)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Objective
This prototype follows the Chapter 19 and 20 workflow from R for Visual Analytics: first understand the monthly time-series pattern, then build and compare forecasting models on a holdout window, and finally refit the best model to generate forward forecasts. The common dataset is organised around monthly visitor arrivals by country, while hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue are used as supporting indicators to interpret whether demand recovery is reflected in tourism performance.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Data Contract
Input dataset: data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx
For this revised forecasting prototype, the analytical scope is intentionally narrowed to monthly visitor-arrival series by country. This matches the instructor guidance and the Chapter 19/20 forecasting workflow more closely than mixing arrivals with hotel or revenue indicators.
Examples of eligible target series are:

Visitor Arrivals: China
Visitor Arrivals: Malaysia
Visitor Arrivals: India
Visitor Arrivals: Indonesia
Visitor Arrivals: Australia
Visitor Arrivals: Japan


tourism_data <- load_tourism_data()
series_catalog <- list_country_arrival_series(tourism_data$long_monthly)
head(series_catalog, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 5
   label                                   unit   n_obs start_date end_date  
   <chr>                                   <chr>  <int> <date>     <date>    
 1 Visitor Arrivals: Australia             Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 2 Visitor Arrivals: Bangladesh            Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 3 Visitor Arrivals: Brunei                Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 4 Visitor Arrivals: Canada                Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 5 Visitor Arrivals: China                 Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 6 Visitor Arrivals: Egypt                 Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 7 Visitor Arrivals: Finland               Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 8 Visitor Arrivals: France                Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
 9 Visitor Arrivals: Germany               Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01
10 Visitor Arrivals: Hong Kong SAR (China) Person   111 2016-12-01 2026-02-01

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Analytical Design
The prototype uses one baseline and one forecasting model:

Baseline: seasonal naive forecast
Forecasting model: auto ARIMA

The workflow is:

Select a monthly series.
Hold out the final 12 months as the test window.
Fit both forecasting approaches on the training data.
Compare RMSE, MAE, and MAPE on the same holdout window.
Visualise actual values and forecasts together.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Example Target Series
Visitor Arrivals: China is used here because it is one of the clearest country-level recovery indicators in the dataset and it shows strong shock, rebound, and seasonal dynamics.

example_label <- "Visitor Arrivals: China"

example_series <- prepare_forecast_series(
  tourism_data$long_monthly,
  example_label
)

forecast_results <- run_modeltime_forecast_workflow(
  series_df = example_series,
  horizon = 12
)

context_series <- prepare_country_context_panel(
  tourism_data$long_monthly,
  country_label = example_label
)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Forecast Plot

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = forecast_results$fitted_df,
    aes(x = date, y = value, color = series),
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = forecast_results$forecast_df,
    aes(x = date, y = value, color = series),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Training" = "#0f6b6f",
      "Test" = "#d86f45",
      "Seasonal Naive" = "#6c757d",
      "ARIMA" = "#7a3e9d"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Forecast Prototype Example",
    subtitle = "Visitor Arrivals: China | 12-month holdout",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Person",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Accuracy Table

DT::datatable(
  forecast_results$accuracy_tbl,
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(dom = "t", pageLength = 5)
)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Output Exposure








Output
Format
Purpose




Context comparison chart
ggplot2
show whether one country-arrivals series moves in tandem with hotel occupancy, stay length, and room revenue


Raw time-series chart
ggplot2
inspect the country-level arrival trend and shock/recovery path


Seasonal chart
ggplot2
compare monthly pattern across years


Decomposition plot
feasts / autoplot
separate trend, seasonality, and remainder


Accuracy table
DT::datatable
compare testing-set metrics


Testing-set forecast plot
modeltime plot
assess model behaviour against actual holdout data


Forward forecast plot
modeltime plot
show the future trajectory after refit

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Quality Gates

The selected series must have at least 24 non-missing monthly observations.
The holdout horizon must leave at least 12 points for model fitting.
A baseline and at least one model-based forecast must be scored on the same testing window.
The forecasting module must use the same source workbook while restricting the target catalog to country-level arrival series.
The workflow must include contextual comparison and visual diagnostics before model comparison.

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to use the final three-module tourism analytics app.

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to use the final three-module tourism analytics app.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 1: Explore the Series
Open the Time Series Explorer tab to:

Choose a monthly series such as Visitor Arrivals: China or Hotel Room Occupancy Rate.
Inspect the recent trend with a line chart.
Confirm the unit and source metadata before moving to deeper analysis.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 2: Compare Recovery States
Open the Cluster Prototype tab to:

Select multiple country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Set the lookback window and choose k.
Decide whether each country trajectory should be normalized before clustering.
Review the scatter plot, silhouette score, and cluster profile table.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 3: Forecast Future Values
Open the Forecasting Prototype tab to:

Select a country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Compare that arrivals series with hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue in the context chart.
Set the holdout horizon.
Choose the forecasting model.
Compare the seasonal-naive baseline with the selected model.
Review the forecast plot and accuracy table.

title: User Guide
text:
Interpretation Tips

Use the explorer first to understand seasonality and structural breaks.
Use clustering second to identify which country-arrival trajectories behave similarly.
Use forecasting last to project one country-level arrivals series while interpreting it against hotel and stay indicators.

title: User Guide
text:
Data Scope
The current app is built on:
data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx
This workbook provides monthly tourism time series for total visitor arrivals, selected countries and regions, hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and selected hotel indicators. In the forecasting module, country-level arrivals are the main forecast target and the hotel/stay indicators provide supporting interpretation.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
The Three Modules

1. Time Series Explorer
Purpose:

Select a monthly tourism series.
Inspect recent years of trend and volatility.
Review source, unit, and series identifier before analysis.



2. Cluster Prototype
Purpose:

Transform monthly observations into recovery-state feature vectors.
Compare state separation across periods such as pre-COVID, shock, and recovery.
Review silhouette quality and profile means.



3. Forecasting Prototype
Purpose:

Select a country-level monthly visitor-arrivals series.
Position that country series against hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue.
Set a train/test horizon.
Compare a seasonal-naive baseline with a forecasting model such as ARIMA or ETS.
Review both the forecast plot and the holdout accuracy table.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Forecasting Workflow
Following the sequence highlighted in Chapter 20, the prototype is organised into seven steps:

Import and inspect the selected monthly country-arrivals series.
Compare that country series with hotel and stay indicators.
Visualise the time path and seasonal structure.
Split the series into training and testing sets.
Fit a baseline and multiple forecasting models.
Compare testing-set accuracy.
Refit the best model and forecast forward.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 1: Visualise the Raw Time Series
text:
Step 1: Visualise the Raw Time Series
Chapter 19 emphasises that time-series work should begin with a direct inspection of trend, turning points, and volatility. The chart below shows the full monthly path of the selected tourism series.

ggplot(example_series, aes(x = date, y = value)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#0f6b6f") +
  geom_point(size = 1.8, color = "#d86f45") +
  labs(
    title = example_label,
    subtitle = "Monthly tourism series used for forecasting",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Step 2: Check Seasonality and Decomposition
The next step is to confirm whether the series contains strong seasonal cycles and how the trend changed around the shock-and-recovery period. This mirrors the Chapter 19 emphasis on seasonal pattern analysis and decomposition.

Seasonal Pattern by Month

example_series |>
  mutate(
    month_lab = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
    year_num = year(date)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month_lab, y = value, group = year_num, color = factor(year_num))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.65) +
  geom_point(size = 1.3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Comparison by Month",
    subtitle = "Each coloured line represents one year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)",
    color = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










STL-style Decomposition

example_tsibble <- example_series |>
  transmute(date = yearmonth(date), value = value) |>
  as_tsibble(index = date)

decomp_tbl <- example_tsibble |>
  model(STL(value)) |>
  components()

autoplot(decomp_tbl) +
  labs(
    title = "Trend / Seasonal / Remainder Decomposition",
    subtitle = "Used to explain the structural change before forecasting"
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 3: Create the Training and Testing Split
text:
Step 3: Create the Training and Testing Split
Following Chapter 20, the final 12 months are reserved as a holdout set. This keeps the evaluation time-aware and avoids random sampling.

split_summary <- tibble(
  segment = c("Training", "Testing"),
  start = c(min(forecast_results$training$date), min(forecast_results$testing$date)),
  end = c(max(forecast_results$training$date), max(forecast_results$testing$date)),
  n_obs = c(nrow(forecast_results$training), nrow(forecast_results$testing))
)

split_summary

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  segment  start      end        n_obs
  <chr>    <date>     <date>     <int>
1 Training 2016-12-01 2025-02-01    99
2 Testing  2025-03-01 2026-02-01    12

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 4: Fit Baseline and Forecasting Models
text:
Step 4: Fit Baseline and Forecasting Models
This prototype includes:

Seasonal Naive as the baseline benchmark.
ETS (Modeltime) using exponential smoothing.
ARIMA using auto_arima through the modeltime workflow.

These model choices match the Chapter 20 recommendation to compare exponential smoothing and ARIMA techniques after building a time-aware train/test split for one selected time series.

forecast_results$models_tbl

# Modeltime Table
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  .model_id .model   .model_desc            
      <int> <list>   <chr>                  
1         1 <fit[+]> ETS(M,AD,M)            
2         2 <fit[+]> ARIMA(0,1,2)(0,0,1)[12]

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 5: Testing-Set Forecast and Accuracy Comparison
text:
Step 5: Testing-Set Forecast and Accuracy Comparison

Accuracy Table

DT::datatable(
  forecast_results$accuracy_tbl,
  rownames = FALSE,
  options = list(dom = "t", pageLength = 6, scrollX = TRUE)
)







Testing Window Forecast Plot

forecast_results$calibration_forecast_tbl |>
  plot_modeltime_forecast(
    .interactive = FALSE,
    .legend_max_width = 25
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Testing-Set Forecast Comparison",
    subtitle = "Seasonal naive is the benchmark; ETS and ARIMA are model-based alternatives"
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 6: Refit the Best Model and Forecast Forward
text:
Step 6: Refit the Best Model and Forecast Forward
After accuracy comparison, the best-performing model is refit on the full series and projected forward for the next 12 months. This is the final step highlighted in Chapter 20.

forecast_results$future_forecast_tbl |>
  plot_modeltime_forecast(
    .interactive = FALSE,
    .legend_max_width = 25
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Forward Forecast After Refit",
    subtitle = "Best model refit on the full monthly series"
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Interpretation Notes
This forecasting prototype now aligns more closely with the course guidance:

It starts with time-series exploration rather than jumping straight to prediction.
It treats the data as an ordered monthly sequence.
It uses a proper holdout split based on time.
It compares a baseline with model-based approaches.
It uses model refitting to produce a future forecast path.

In presentation terms, the recommended narrative is:

Show the country-arrivals series together with hotel and stay indicators to position the source-market recovery in a broader tourism context.
Use the raw trend, seasonal plot, and decomposition to justify a forecasting approach.
Compare Seasonal Naive, ETS, and ARIMA on the same testing window.
Conclude with the best model and the forward projection.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Analytical Framing
The forecasting module uses a two-layer logic:

Core forecasting series: country-level monthly visitor arrivals.
Supporting performance indicators: hotel room occupancy rate, average length of stay, and total room revenue.

This means the forecasts answer the question “how may demand from each source market evolve?” while the supporting indicators help answer “does that demand recovery translate into broader tourism performance?”

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 1: Position the Country Series Within Tourism Performance
text:
Step 1: Position the Country Series Within Tourism Performance
Before forecasting, the selected country-arrivals series should be interpreted together with hotel and stay indicators. The values are normalized so the focus stays on shared turning points rather than raw units.

ggplot(context_series, aes(x = date, y = normalized_value, color = label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Country Arrivals Compared with Supporting Tourism Indicators",
    subtitle = "Normalized z-scores show whether demand recovery aligns with occupancy, stay length, and room revenue",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Normalized z-score",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 2: Visualise the Raw Time Series
text:
Step 2: Visualise the Raw Time Series
Chapter 19 emphasises that time-series work should begin with a direct inspection of trend, turning points, and volatility. The chart below shows the full monthly path of the selected country-arrivals series.

ggplot(example_series, aes(x = date, y = value)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#0f6b6f") +
  geom_point(size = 1.8, color = "#d86f45") +
  labs(
    title = example_label,
    subtitle = "Monthly country-level visitor arrivals used for forecasting",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Step 3: Check Seasonality and Decomposition
The next step is to confirm whether the series contains strong seasonal cycles and how the trend changed around the shock-and-recovery period. This mirrors the Chapter 19 emphasis on seasonal pattern analysis and decomposition.

  mutate(

  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Comparison by Month",
    subtitle = "Each coloured line represents one year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)",
    color = "Year"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










STL-style Decomposition

example_tsibble <- example_series |>
  transmute(date = yearmonth(date), value = value) |>
  as_tsibble(index = date)

decomp_tbl <- example_tsibble |>
  model(STL(value)) |>
  components()

autoplot(decomp_tbl) +
  labs(
    title = "Trend / Seasonal / Remainder Decomposition",
    subtitle = "Used to explain the structural change before forecasting"
  )
